file_name: Gayatri and Aarthy - 2023 - Reduction of overfitting on the highly imbalanced ISIC-2019 skin dataset using deep learning framewo.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses deep learning frameworks, specifically a fine-tuned ResNet-50 model, to perform multi-class classification of skin cancer lesions using the ISIC-2019 dataset. It employs data augmentation and focal loss to address class imbalance and overfitting, achieving 98.85% accuracy.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the application of deep learning frameworks (specifically fine-tuned ResNet-50) to the ISIC-2019 dataset. It details a specific methodology including data augmentation and focal loss to address class imbalance and overfitting, and reports new quantitative results (e.g., 98.85% accuracy). Although it includes a 'Related works' section, its primary purpose is reporting new experimental findings rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Reduction of overfitting on the highly imbalanced ISIC-2019 skin dataset using deep learning frameworks
journal: Journal of X-Ray Science and Technology
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of class imbalance and overfitting in multi-class skin cancer classification using the highly imbalanced ISIC-2019 dataset. The authors employed a fine-tuned ResNet-50 deep learning framework, implementing data augmentation and focal loss techniques to mitigate these issues. The model was evaluated on eight classes of skin lesions, including melanoma and basal cell carcinoma, using metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, and Matthews Correlation Coefficient. The combination of augmented data and focal loss significantly reduced the overfitting gap between training and validation accuracy. The proposed approach achieved a high classification performance of 98.85% accuracy, 95.52% precision, and 95.93% recall. The results demonstrate that focal loss effectively handles hard misclassified samples, improving the overall diagnostic capability for imbalanced medical image data.

all_task_types.target: Multi-class skin cancer diagnosis (8 classes: Actinic Keratosis, Benign Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Dermatofibroma, Melanoma, Melanocytic Nevus, Squamous Cell Carcinoma, Vascular Lesion)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multi-class classification of skin lesions using the ISIC-2019 dataset. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories for eight specific skin lesion types. Performance is evaluated using classification metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and Matthews Correlation Coefficient (MCC).

datasets_used.name: ISIC-2019
datasets_used.description: A public dermoscopic image dataset from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) containing 8 classes of skin lesions. The dataset consists of 25,331 total images.

use.training: Used for training the ResNet-50 model. In the first run, 20,265 images were used for training. In the second run (with augmentation), 23,085 images were used for training. In the third run (with focal loss on original data), 20,265 images were used for training. In the fourth run (with augmentation and focal loss), the augmented dataset was used for training.
use.validation: Used for validation. In the first run, 5,066 images were used for validation. In the second run (with augmentation), 5,771 images were used for validation.
use.testing: Used for testing/evaluation. Class-wise testing counts are provided in Tables 3 and 5 (e.g., AK: 173, BCC: 664, BKL: 525, DF: 48, Melanoma: 904, Nevus: 2575, SCC: 126, VASC: 51).
use.other: The dataset was subjected to data augmentation (rotation and flipping) for specific classes (AK, DF, SCC, VASC) to address class imbalance. The total number of images after augmentation was 28,856.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made using descriptive statistics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, MCC) across different experimental runs (original data, augmented data, focal loss, and combined) without statistical significance testing.

ResNet-50.notes: Average overfitting gap between training and validation accuracy was around 13%.

Run 1: Original Data (ResNet-50).notes: First run performed to assess overfitting on original ISIC-2019 dataset. 20,265 images for training, 5,066 for validation. 100 iterations, learning rate varied from 0.1 to 0.01, weight decay 0.00001.

ResNet-50.notes: Data augmentation (Rotate-90, 180, 270 and vertical flip) applied to AK, DF, SCC, VASC. Total images: 28,856. 23,085 for training, 5,771 for validation. Average overfitting gap reduced to 11%.

Run 2: Augmented Data (ResNet-50).notes: Second run used data augmentation to balance classes. Classes BCC, BKL, Melanoma, Nevus were not augmented.

ResNet-50.notes: Focal loss applied to original data. Average overfitting gap reduced to 4.9%.

Run 3: Original Data + Focal Loss (ResNe.notes: Third run applied focal loss to original ISIC-2019 data to handle class imbalance.

ResNet-50.notes: Combined data augmentation and focal loss. Average overfitting gap reduced to 3%.

Run 4: Augmented Data + Focal Loss (ResN.notes: Fourth run implemented focal loss with augmented data. This configuration yielded the best performance.

Comparison with Previous Studies.notes: Comparison of ResNet-50 performance with previous studies listed in Table 8.

architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained on ImageNet dataset (1.5M natural scenery images)
architecture_used.input_size: 7x7 input layer followed by 3x3 kernel size pooling layer (specific image dimensions not explicitly stated, but ISIC-2019 images are dermoscopic RGB)

architecture_details.hardware: MSI GF-65 gaming laptop, Intel i7-10gen,750H, NVIDIA GeForce RTX-3060 6GB

techniques: Data augmentation (Rotate-90, 180, 270 and vertical flip for specific classes)

classification_or_output_head.details: Softmax used to avoid non-linearity in output and estimate multinomial probability for each class

preprocessing_techniques: Data augmentation via rotation (90, 180, 270 degrees) and vertical flipping applied to specific classes (Actinic Keratosis, Dermatofibroma, Squamous Cell Carcinoma, Vascular Lesion) to address class imbalance.
80/20 train-validation split of the ISIC-2019 dataset.
Use of pre-trained weights from ImageNet dataset for the ResNet-50 model.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to specific classes: Actinic Keratosis (AK) rotated 90 degrees; Dermatofibroma (DF) rotated 90, 180, and 270 degrees; Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC) rotated 90 and 180 degrees; Vascular Lesion (VASC) rotated 90, 180, and 270 degrees.
Applied to specific classes: Dermatofibroma (DF) and Vascular Lesion (VASC).

conclusions: The combination of data augmentation and focal loss effectively reduces overfitting in highly imbalanced skin cancer classification tasks, significantly narrowing the gap between training and validation accuracy.
The proposed fine-tuned ResNet-50 model with augmented data and focal loss achieved superior performance on the ISIC-2019 dataset, with 98.85% accuracy, 95.52% precision, and 95.93% recall.
Focal loss is identified as an effective technique for handling class imbalance by assigning weights to hard misclassified samples, thereby improving classification accuracy for minority classes.
Matthews Correlation Coefficient (MCC) is recommended as the best metric for evaluating the quality of multi-class medical image classification, particularly in the presence of class imbalance.

The study demonstrates that deep learning frameworks, specifically ResNet-50, can support dermatologists in the early and automated detection of skin cancer lesions, addressing the challenge of visual similarity between benign and malignant lesions.
The authors acknowledge that future work will extend the proposed methods to different types of pre-trained models for classification.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the ISIC-2019 dataset without external validation, limiting generalizability to other populations or imaging devices.
Data augmentation was applied only to minority classes (AK, DF, SCC, VASC), while majority classes (BCC, BKL, Melanoma, Nevus) remained un-augmented, potentially introducing bias.
The paper does not report any statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values) for performance comparisons between models or runs.
The hardware used for training was a consumer-grade laptop (NVIDIA GeForce RTX-3060 6GB), which may limit reproducibility for larger-scale deployments.

The model achieved very high validation accuracy (98.85%) with a minimal overfitting gap (3%), but the small validation set size (5,771 images) may not robustly represent the full dataset distribution.
Class weights for focal loss were manually assigned (e.g., Nevus weight 0.1, DF weight 1.0) without reported justification for specific values, which could affect reproducibility.
The paper compares results with previous studies (Refs [19], [25], [26], [27], [28]) but does not provide detailed methodology for these external baselines, making direct comparison difficult.
